summary:
Claim: Juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma (JNA) does not follow standard oncological staging but is informally categorized by extent of spread. When extending into one or more paranasal sinuses, it is typically described as progressing from localized disease to advanced local invasion.  

Evidence: - No formal AJCC or TNM staging exists for JNA; staging relies on clinical judgment and anatomical extension. - Commonly, progression includes Stage I (localized), Stage II (extension into sinuses), and Stage III (skull base/orbit invasion), though this is not internationally validated.  

Answer: B (Stage II) — as sinus extension represents intermediate advancement beyond localized disease, consistent with typical clinical classification patterns, despite lacking official recognition.  

(498 tokens)

Claim: Juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma does not follow standard oncological staging but is informally categorized by extent of spread.  
Evidence: - No formal AJCC or TNM staging system applies to angiofibroma; classification depends on clinical and anatomical features. - Clinically, extension into paranasal sinuses is generally considered Stage II, preceding deeper skull-base invasion.  
Answer: B
